details.body: Fix stale writer-lock recovery before `run resume`/`run stop` rely on the new PID-based stale-run flow. `handle_resume` still acquires the CLI writer lease before any stale-Running reconciliation (`src/cli/run.rs:1769-1778`), and `CliWriterLeaseGuard::acquire` hard-fails on an existing lock (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:136-166`). The new stale-stop/interruption paths only rewrite `run.json`/`run.pid` (`src/cli/run.rs:1885-1941`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:729-757`) and never release or reclaim the old writer lock. After a SIGKILL/OOM/crash, the run is reported as stale, but `run resume` can still fail with `ProjectWriterLockHeld` until daemon TTL/reconcile clears the stale lease. Add lease cleanup/reclamation to stale-run recovery, or move the stale detection ahead of lock acquisition so the recovery path can clear the stranded lock before trying to resume.

Remove the startup race where a live run can be misclassified as stale before `run.pid` exists. The engine writes `run.json` as `Running` before creating the pid file on both start and resume (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1080-1115`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1806-1849`), but `run status` and `run stop` immediately treat a missing pid file as proof that the orchestrator is dead (`src/cli/run.rs:1885-1899`, `src/cli/run.rs:5319-5338`). In that window, `run status` reports a healthy run as stale, and `run stop` can mark the snapshot failed without ever signaling the still-running orchestrator. Either write the pid file before exposing `Running`, or make `status`/`stop` tolerate the pre-pid startup window and/or coordinate with the writer lease.

Teach resume to recover a run that dies after `run.json` is written but before `run_started` reaches the journal. The start path exposes a `Running` snapshot before `run_started` is durable (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1095-1106`). If the orchestrator is killed in that window, stale-run reconciliation turns the snapshot into `Failed`, but resume still hard-requires a `run_started` entry in both `stage_plan_for_resume` and `derive_resume_state` (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6455-6464` and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6489-6498`). The result is that `run resume` still aborts with "run journal does not contain a run_started event" for one of the core process-death windows. Add a fallback that reconstructs the resume state from `interrupted_run`/`active_run`, or make startup journal durability happen before the `Running` snapshot is published.

Stop deleting `run.pid` just because the snapshot is not yet `Running`. `run stop` currently removes the pid file in the non-running fast path (`src/cli/run.rs:1911-1914`), while both start and resume now write `run.pid` before they persist the `Running` snapshot (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1090-1098` and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1809-1815`). A concurrent `run stop` in that startup window will return "not currently running" but still delete the live orchestrator's pid record, after which `run status`/`run resume` misclassify the still-running process as stale. Only clear `run.pid` after you have proven the recorded process is dead, or coordinate `run stop` with startup via the writer lease.

Make `run stop` verify that the SIGKILL path actually terminated the process before it rewrites state and reclaims locks. After the SIGTERM grace period, the code sends SIGKILL and discards the result of the follow-up liveness wait (`src/cli/run.rs:1962-1964`), then immediately reclaims the writer lease and may mark the snapshot failed as if the run were stopped (`src/cli/run.rs:1967-1986`). On Unix a successful `kill(SIGKILL)` does not guarantee the process is gone immediately, especially if it is stuck in uninterruptible I/O, so this can split-brain the project by releasing the lock and rewriting `run.json` while the orchestrator is still alive. If the post-SIGKILL liveness check still says the pid is alive, `run stop` should fail and leave recovery state untouched.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5649-5664` converts a resumable completion blocker into a failed snapshot path (`snapshot.status = RunStatus::Failed; snapshot.active_run = None;`), writes it, then returns early without calling `FileSystem::remove_pid_file`. This leaves `run.pid` behind after a terminal non-running outcome. Same function therefore violates PID cleanup in a normal terminal failure mode (`complete_guard`), which should always delete the orchestrator liveness file.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5667-5684` in `complete_run` writes a completed snapshot first, then appends `run_completed` with `?`; if that append fails, the function returns before reaching `remove_pid_file`, and the PID file is never removed. If the snapshot write succeeds, the run is marked `Completed` in durable state while the completion event is absent and the PID liveness file remains.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:749-752` still deletes `run.pid` whenever `mark_running_run_interrupted()` rereads a non-`Running` snapshot. That reintroduces the same startup-window bug this patch was trying to close: stale recovery is triggered before the writer lease is reacquired (`src/cli/run.rs:1769-1781`), while resume republishes `run.pid` before it writes the new `Running` snapshot (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1820-1827`). Two concurrent `run resume`/`run stop` attempts against the same stale snapshot can therefore race so that the loser observes the winner's intermediate `Failed` snapshot and deletes the winner's freshly written pid file, after which `run status` and later recovery misclassify a live orchestrator as stale.

`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6470-6476` and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6500-6593` only fall back to `snapshot.interrupted_run` when the journal has no `run_started` event at all. If the journal contains an older visible `run_started`/terminal pair, `run resume` binds to that old run id and cursor instead of the recovered snapshot metadata. A recovered stale-start snapshot can then fail with `all stages in the current flow are already complete` or resume the wrong run. I reproduced this by seeding `run.json` with `interrupted_run.run_id = run-current` and a journal containing an older `run_started`/`run_completed` for `run-old`; `ralph-burning run resume` exited with the bogus `already complete` error instead of resuming `run-current`.

Do not write `run.pid` from the shared engine for daemon-dispatched runs. `execute_run_with_retry_internal()` and `resume_run_with_retry_internal()` always persist `std::process::id()` into the pid file (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1100`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1829`), but the daemon calls those same entry points inline inside the long-lived daemon process (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:3203`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:3224`). That means pid liveness now tracks the daemon process, not the per-project run task: a dead/stuck daemon-owned run will still look "alive" while the daemon stays up, and `run stop` will send SIGTERM/SIGKILL to the whole daemon (`src/cli/run.rs:1956-1962`) instead of only stopping the target run. The pid/liveness design needs to distinguish CLI-owned orchestrators from daemon-owned task execution before this is safe.

Make stale-run recovery atomic with respect to the writer lock. `handle_resume()` rewrites a stale Running snapshot via `recover_stale_running_snapshot()` before it acquires a new `CliWriterLeaseGuard` (`src/cli/run.rs:1769-1814`), and `recover_stale_running_snapshot()` immediately calls `reclaim_stale_project_writer_lease()` (`src/cli/run.rs:1592-1609`). That helper blindly releases whatever writer lock is present and deletes every CLI lease for the project (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:62-98`). If two operators race `run resume`/`run stop`, one process can acquire a fresh writer lock between the snapshot rewrite and the reclaim step, and the late reclaim will tear down that live lock/lease, leaving both callers mutating run state concurrently. Recovery needs to claim ownership atomically before deleting locks, or the reclaim helper must verify staleness instead of removing the current owner unconditionally.

Persist a durable terminal journal event when `run stop` or stale-run recovery converts a Running snapshot into a resumable Failed state. `mark_running_run_interrupted()` only rewrites `run.json` and appends a runtime log (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:740-777`), and the stop path just calls that helper (`src/cli/run.rs:1979-1988`). `run history` is built from the journal only (`src/cli/run.rs:5403-5410`), so after `run stop` the durable history has no `run_failed`/interruption event for the stopped run even though the snapshot says Failed. That leaves audit/history/reconciliation consumers with an incomplete view of what happened.

`run stop` can tear down a brand-new CLI run that wins the handoff after the old orchestrator exits. In `src/cli/run.rs:2052-2057` the stop path re-reads the current writer-lock owner after waiting for the target PID to exit, then `acquire_recovery_writer_guard` immediately calls `reclaim_specific_cli_writer_lease` (`src/cli/run.rs:1535-1542`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:85-113`) without proving that owner is stale. If another `run start` or `run resume` acquires the lock in that window, `run stop` steals its live lease; the fallback branch at `src/cli/run.rs:2085-2088` then deletes whichever `run.pid` is present, which can orphan the new run. Capture the owner before sending SIGTERM/SIGKILL and only reclaim that exact stale lease, or otherwise verify the replacement owner is dead before releasing its lock or pid file.

`run stop` stores `proc_start_ticks` to avoid PID reuse, but it does not use that information when delivering signals. The stop path only does a one-time liveness check during classification, then calls `kill` on the raw PID in `src/cli/run.rs:2034-2041` via `send_signal_to_pid` (`src/cli/run.rs:1612-1619`). If the orchestrator exits and the PID is recycled before SIGTERM/SIGKILL is sent, `run stop` can signal an unrelated process. Revalidate the `RunPidRecord` against `/proc/<pid>/stat` immediately before each signal (or make the signal helper reject mismatched `proc_start_ticks`) instead of trusting the numeric PID alone.

Daemon-owned runs still do not have real crash detection. The daemon always invokes `execute_run_with_retry` / `resume_run_with_retry` with `Some(worktree_path)` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:3203-3235`), and the engine explicitly removes `run.pid` instead of writing one in that mode (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1145-1153`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1888-1896`). `run status` / `run resume` then treat any fresh worktree lease as alive (`src/cli/run.rs:1524-1529`). A hard-killed daemon task therefore stays `running` until the lease TTL expires, and `run resume` keeps refusing during that window. Either persist a real liveness marker for daemon-owned orchestrators too, or make status/resume consult an actual daemon-task/process health signal rather than lease freshness.

`classify_running_snapshot_liveness` now treats any running snapshot with no `run.pid` as stale (`src/cli/run.rs:1518-1535`), and `run resume` / `run stop` immediately reclaim that owner in the stale path (`src/cli/run.rs:1833-1900`, `src/cli/run.rs:2007-2045`). That breaks mixed-version rollouts for daemon-owned runs: daemon tasks that were already in flight before this change have no pid file yet, so the new binary will misclassify a still-live daemon run as stale and can steal its writer lock. Add a compatibility path for preexisting daemon runs instead of treating missing pid as stale unconditionally.

Stale daemon recovery now deletes the observed `WorktreeLease` directly in `reclaim_specific_project_writer_owner` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:119-152`), and `run resume` / `run stop` call that path before recovering the snapshot (`src/cli/run.rs:1875-1900`, `src/cli/run.rs:2013-2044`). That bypasses the daemon reconcile/release flow that marks the daemon task failed and clears `task.lease_id` (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/lease_service.rs:473-491`, `src/contexts/automation_runtime/lease_service.rs:601-608`). `DaemonTaskService::retry_task` still rejects tasks with a retained lease reference (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/task_service.rs:499-505`), so a crashed daemon task can be left permanently wedged after CLI recovery. Route stale daemon recovery through the normal daemon cleanup path or clear/update the daemon task record alongside the lease reclamation.

`run stop` still has a PID-reuse race. `send_signal_to_pid` checks `FileSystem::is_pid_alive(record)` and then sends `kill()` to the raw PID (`src/cli/run.rs:1624-1638`), and the stop flow uses that helper for both SIGTERM and SIGKILL (`src/cli/run.rs:2055-2064`). If the orchestrator exits after the liveness check and the PID is recycled before `kill()` runs, `run stop` can signal an unrelated process. The current `proc_start_ticks` check is only a userspace preflight; it does not make the signal delivery itself safe. Use an OS handle that remains bound to the original process (for example a pidfd on Linux) or another atomic mechanism before claiming PID-reuse is handled.

The patch is metadata-only and does not implement the requested run-lifecycle hardening contract in runtime code. The only file-level changes are in `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/journal.ndjson` (new `reviewer_started` entries at lines 113-114) and `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/run.json` (added `stage_resolution_snapshot`/metadata under `active_run` at lines 16-37), while the behavior files required by the goal (`src/adapters/fs.rs`, `src/cli/run.rs`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`) are untouched by this diff.

Extend `run resume` stale-owner recovery beyond `RunStatus::Running`. In `src/cli/run.rs:1983-2038`, the code only captures/reclaims an observed writer owner when the snapshot still says `running`, but the CLI does not actually close its `CliWriterLeaseGuard` until much later (`src/cli/run.rs:1950-1953` and `src/cli/run.rs:2130-2132`) after post-run sync. If the orchestrator dies after the engine has already written a resumable `failed`/`paused` snapshot but before `close()` runs, the orphaned `writer-*.lock` survives and the next `run resume` goes straight into `CliWriterLeaseGuard::acquire`, which fails with `ProjectWriterLockHeld`. Reclaim or ignore stale writer owners for resumable snapshots too, otherwise SIGTERM/OOM during post-run cleanup still wedges the run.

Do not treat `run.pid` records without `proc_start_ticks` as authoritative liveness. `FileSystem::is_pid_alive` in `src/adapters/fs.rs:329-347` returns true for any live PID when `proc_start_ticks` is `None`, `classify_running_snapshot_liveness` in `src/cli/run.rs:1531-1539` uses that to block `status`/`resume`, and the non-Linux stop path in `src/cli/run.rs:1705-1710` plus `src/cli/run.rs:1740-1753` will signal that PID directly. After PID reuse, a stale or legacy `run.pid` can therefore make an unrelated process look like the orchestrator, and on non-Linux `run stop` can send SIGTERM/SIGKILL to the wrong process. Treat missing `proc_start_ticks` as unsafe/stale everywhere, or add a platform-specific equivalent start-time check before trusting the PID.

Filter `run status` reconciliation by the current run id before applying terminal journal events. Right now `handle_status` unconditionally calls `reconcile_snapshot_status` on the full journal (`src/cli/run.rs:5655-5662`), and `reconcile_snapshot_status` uses the journal's global last terminal event (`src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:24-45`). If the current stale `Running` snapshot belongs to a newer run that died before its `run_started` reached disk, but the journal still ends with an older `run_completed`/`run_failed`, `run status` will report the old terminal state instead of `stale` or the active run's state.

Make stale-run detection safe for pre-upgrade CLI runs that do not have the new authoritative pid tuple yet. `FileSystem::is_pid_alive` returns `false` whenever `proc_start_ticks` is missing (`src/adapters/fs.rs:329-347`), and `classify_running_snapshot_liveness` also treats a fresh `CliWriter` lease without a pid marker as `Stale` unconditionally (`src/cli/run.rs:1530-1563`). `handle_resume` and `handle_stop` then reclaim the writer lock and rewrite run state on that basis (`src/cli/run.rs:2040-2058`, `src/cli/run.rs:2172-2204`). For a live older CLI orchestrator, that can start a second orchestrator or mark the run failed without ever stopping the original process.

When `run stop` escalates to SIGKILL, terminate the active backend subprocess tree as well as the orchestrator. The new stop path only signals the orchestrator PID (`src/cli/run.rs:2219-2242`), but process-backend invocations are launched in their own process groups (`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1122-1126`). A forced stop can therefore kill the orchestrator while leaving the backend child group running, after which the command marks the snapshot failed and ready to resume.

Preserve journal-terminal outcomes before stale-owner recovery in `run resume`/`run stop`. `src/cli/run.rs:2117-2132`, `src/cli/run.rs:2263-2277`, and `src/cli/run.rs:2349-2363` now unconditionally call `recover_stale_running_snapshot` once the writer lease is claimed. That bypasses the existing journal-first reconciliation in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1449-1504`, which is the code that distinguishes 'snapshot write missed a durable run_failed' from 'snapshot write missed a durable run_completed'. If the previous orchestrator already appended `run_completed` or `run_failed` and only the snapshot write failed, these new CLI paths will rewrite the run to `Failed` with an interruption/cancellation summary, and `run resume` can then restart a run that should have stayed terminal.

Make status reconciliation attempt-aware before trusting terminal journal events. The updated `reconcile_snapshot_status` in `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:27-40` now filters by `run_id`, but resumed attempts reuse the same run id (`tests/unit/workflow_engine_test.rs:3792-3800`). A live resumed run therefore still has an older `run_failed` for that same `run_id`, and `handle_status` (`src/cli/run.rs:5744-5749`) will incorrectly patch the current Running snapshot to Failed/Completed even though the resumed attempt is still active. The reconciliation needs to ignore terminal events that occurred before the most recent `run_started`/`run_resumed` boundary for the current attempt.

Handle crashes that occur after a resumed attempt writes `run.json`/`run.pid` but before `run_resumed` is appended. In `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:31-59`, `terminal_status_for_running_attempt()` requires a durable `run_started`/`run_resumed` boundary and then trusts the last terminal event after that boundary. If the process dies in the resume window before `run_resumed` is durable, the helper falls back to the original `run_started`, so an older `run_failed` from the previous attempt is treated as the current outcome. `run status` (`src/cli/run.rs:5885-5890`) then reports `failed`/`completed` instead of `stale`, and the same helper also feeds stale stop/resume reconciliation. Use the active snapshot metadata (for example `active_run.started_at`) as a fallback boundary for an unjournaled resumed attempt, and add a regression test for 'crash after resume snapshot write, before run_resumed append'.

Do not make stale-run recovery depend on a leftover writer-lock owner file. In `src/cli/run.rs:2211-2235`, the new pre-recovery path only runs when `stale_recovery_owner.is_some()`. If the stale writer lock was already cleaned up, `run resume` skips reconciliation and falls back to `engine::resume_run_with_retry()`, whose `RunStatus::Running` branch in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1452-1481` still just returns 'already completed per journal' for a durable `run_completed` event without rewriting `run.json`. That leaves the on-disk snapshot stuck at `status = "running"` even after `run resume` proves the run is terminal. Once the CLI has acquired the fresh writer lease, it should reconcile any stale Running snapshot regardless of whether an old owner string was present, and there should be a regression test for a stale completed run with no writer-lock files remaining.

Patch does not include any runtime implementation for the requested lifecycle hardening. `git diff` shows changes only in `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/run.json` (`lines 15-37`, `52-72`) and `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/journal.ndjson` (`lines 186-189`) and adds no edits to `src/adapters/fs.rs`, `src/cli/run.rs`, or `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`. Therefore required behaviors for `run.pid` tracking, `run stop`, stale `run status` reporting, stale-run recovery in `run resume`, and signal-based interruption cleanup are not delivered by this change set.

Update `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1448-1478` to use the new attempt-scoped reconciliation logic instead of `journal::last_terminal_event_type(&running_run_events)`. The helper added in `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs:33-85` fixes resumed runs that crash after writing `run.json` but before durably appending `run_resumed`, but the core engine resume path still inspects the last terminal event for the whole `run_id` and can therefore inherit the previous attempt's `run_failed`/`run_completed`. Direct callers of `engine::resume_run_with_retry` can still misclassify that stale `Running` snapshot and either reject resume or reconcile it to the wrong terminal state. Add an engine-level regression test for this unjournaled-resume case.

The patch does not implement the requested lifecycle hardening at all: it only mutates metadata artifacts (`.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/run.json:1-74` and `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/journal.ndjson:195-208`) and contains no hunks in the required implementation files (`src/adapters/fs.rs`, `src/cli/run.rs`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`). Acceptance criteria items (pid tracking helpers, `run stop` command behavior, stale status detection, resume-side stale recovery, and SIGTERM-driven cleanup) are therefore not addressed in this change set.

Guard the new stale-running reconciliation path in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:1453-1502` against clobbering a newer run. Both new branches call `mark_running_run_interrupted()`, but that helper rereads `run.json` and unconditionally rewrites any still-`Running` snapshot at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:755-782` without checking that it is the same `(run_id, started_at)` attempt that was just classified as stale. Two overlapping `run resume` invocations can therefore race: one invocation starts a fresh run, while the slower invocation still holding the stale preflight view marks that new run as `Failed`, removes its pid file, and may append a bogus interruption event. Thread the expected attempt identity into `mark_running_run_interrupted` (or do a compare-and-swap before rewriting) and add a regression that simulates concurrent resumes.

Make current-version pid records authoritative on non-Linux Unix. `write_pid_file` records `proc_start_ticks: None` whenever `/proc/<pid>/stat` is unavailable (`src/adapters/fs.rs:284-295`, `src/adapters/fs.rs:670-680`), and both `is_pid_alive` and `classify_running_snapshot_liveness` reject those records as non-authoritative (`src/adapters/fs.rs:329-347`, `src/cli/run.rs:1533-1547`). On macOS/BSD that means every current run falls back to the legacy lease path, so a dead orchestrator keeps reporting as running until the lease TTL expires, `run resume` refuses stale recovery (`src/cli/run.rs:1618-1624`), and `run stop` rejects the run as a legacy CLI run (`src/cli/run.rs:2378-2379`). Add a non-`/proc` process identity check for non-Linux Unix, or another way to mark current pid records as authoritative there.

Do not reclaim an observed writer-lock owner for ordinary `Failed`/`Paused` resumes unless that owner has been proven stale. `observed_resume_recovery_owner` records whatever lock owner exists even for terminal snapshots (`src/cli/run.rs:1635-1637`), and `acquire_recovery_writer_guard` unconditionally calls `reclaim_specific_project_writer_owner` before trying to acquire its own lease (`src/cli/run.rs:1579-1594`, `src/cli/run.rs:2230-2238`). If another `run resume` or daemon worker wins the race after we read `run.json`, this path will tear down that live owner and can even mark the live worktree task failed (`src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:123-175`) before we later discover the snapshot is already `Running`. Reclaim should only happen after stale-owner proof, or after a normal acquire fails and the existing owner is independently verified stale.

`git diff` for this revision only touches `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/journal.ndjson` (around lines 237–243) and `.ralph-burning/projects/rlm/run.json` (around lines 12–29). No source files under `src/` are modified, so required runtime changes for PID lifecycle tracking (`src/adapters/fs.rs`), `run stop`/stale handling (`src/cli/run.rs`), and resume recovery/signal-safe interruption (`src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`) are not implemented in this patch.

`src/cli/run.rs:1579-1655` regresses post-run cleanup crash recovery. `acquire_recovery_writer_guard()` now refuses to reclaim the observed writer owner unless its lease has already gone stale, but `complete_run`/`pause_run`/`fail_run` remove `run.pid` before the writer lease is released in `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5796`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5819`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5841`, and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5933`. If the CLI dies in that cleanup window, the snapshot is already terminal and there is no pid file left to prove the process is gone, so `run resume` stays blocked for the full lease TTL even though nothing is running. Recovery needs a fresh-death path for stranded CLI cleanup locks instead of forcing users to wait for lease expiry.

`src/adapters/fs.rs:333-361` plus `src/adapters/fs.rs:702-745` now treat `ps -o lstart` as an authoritative process identity on non-Linux Unix, and `src/cli/run.rs:1804-1853` uses that identity to send SIGTERM/SIGKILL during `run stop`. `lstart` is only second-granularity text, so a recycled PID started within the same second can satisfy the check and let `run stop` target an unrelated process. Until there is a stronger per-process identifier on these platforms, pid records backed only by `proc_start_marker` should not be considered authoritative for stop/resume decisions.
